From this day I could first consider myself as being appointed by his Majesty on the Expedition. The Order signed by the King imported that 1795. I should be paid to me by the Exchequer, which I receive accordingly some says afterwards.

the same day I went to Mr. Banks & not finding him at home I wrote a note to him desiring him to appoint me a day, when I could have the favour to speak to him; for being appointed by his Majesty to go on the Expedition, I wished to be informed by him to me particulars relative to my equipment & having always been favoured with his friendship I hoped, he would add this to the many obligations he had already, laid me under as it was just going away Dr Scharter came in, I told of my going & began likewise his advice, He congratulated are coolly & was civil; but the moment

Name to see W. Banks & begged of him the favour

went to the Treasury & Exchequer & got 179 5. E pei

20th all my baggage was sent on board the

June 4th String completed my Equipment. I took leave
by friends
of my Family, & took a post-things for Plymouth,
The Resolution having left to Spearness y 22°.
We passed through Brenton a dirty town, came to
Aounslow, all a fine & cultivated country, then
passed Avaslow Heath, filled with furge & gallous
both a disgrace to the Country. Dined at Stain-
bridge: passed over the uncultivated hills on
Baghd common, which furnishy London Mac->
rets with the finest mutton, but in my o-
pinion would be better employed in plm->

pinion would be better employed in plm->
tations chiefly of oak. The same country con-
tinues to Marcel green, where we went to
Basingstock an ancient town; Immunology
we passed Sutton & Stockbridge, & having closed
the Sleak & crazy health of the plains
of salisbury, we reached that ancient city.
the Spire & remarkable on account of
& has walls from the city
its' height & pointed figure & Our mild' law
still on the same Warren Heath,
The bottom of all the country is chalk.

Pame ye 27. We reached Barnet-down a single house & then
came to Blandford. The country begins to grow hill
between the latter place & Doschester, a tolerable
good place. All Dosetshire is well cultivated, &
from the summits of its hills, it bears a 1 mg.
charming aspect. At Bridport the Sea comes up
is in
within two miles, The town, let a most thriving
condition & has a manufacture of Sailists
which employs a good number of hand.
On account of the vicinity of the Sea, the top
of the hills were this day codered with clouds
which soon were resolved into rain. We
in Deponshire

reached Axmin for white carpets are many
factured; from this place we sit out for Honito
a populous borough at the bottom of a pale
Its chief house commodity are course cloaths.
Exeter the next city, is a very busy cable dinner
ty place, surrounded by old decaying walls,
its streets are extremely narrow, go up
& down hill, & make the city to a firm all
plare. The front of its cathidra has a great

but the villages are now quite disagree

June 1928. We passed some very high hills in gay to Chudeigh a small incomprehensible town The same aspect of the country is between the latter place & Ashburton another small town. The Inn we put up, It was a pied by a Scotsman called Kenneth A who overcharged every article of our bad fare, & gave us most sorry horses. & a surely, impudent driver. At toy bridge, are a few houses & two inns, one on each side the bridge. That which lie on the casside has the better appearance but

but the landlady on the western side was a sensible old woman. In the Afternoon over reached Plymouth. All the cov. aty of Poonshire is pretty well cultivated in its dalei, as far at the high sleep of the numerous hills will permit. The Harbandry seems however to be rather neglected: for all that, it bears a most roma-
Fio aspect, from the top of the higher hill. We enquired immediately for the Resolution & heard She was not yet arrived. Plymouth j. irveyula
a place with narrow winding trees, which Breh I

of one such

& from which towards mount Edgecombe a Ledge of rocks extends. This Isle is so & is a mere rock with a few houses, in which a gunner & a few soldiers vice. Here Mr. Deiley that stop. norner, who was intended to do on the Expe dition, in the Adventure, had erected his ten & made observations of equal Altitudes, & after wards when the Resolution arrived Mr. Wales joined him with the Transit-instrument, & they settled both the Latitude & Longitude of this place-vig. July ye ye 1st went on board the Augusta-Shackt, to was upon Lord Sandwich, who had desired Capi-sim-> the day before neaux, to let me know he wanted to speak to me. His Lordship received me in the most obliging manner; after breakfast, he took me on the poop of the Vacht, & asked me respectively, whether he could do any this more for me, & thanked his Lordship for his kindness

ness & told, that I thought my present
situation so happy, that no other wish
was left to me than, that this
worship would be pleased to continue to me his
protection & favour. He said he expected
the Resolution that night in Plymouth
sound & in that case, wished to see
me on board of her, between five
& Sir. After having conversed with his
aordship for about 4 of & hour. he
went in his large to the dock-yard
& took me along with him, & went
through the whole yard with me & whether
he thought an explication of the various objects to works might
A necessary, he was pleased to give

A necessary, he was pleased to give
me the fullest Information of every
thing. after one o'clock His Lochip
went on board the barge & recommend
me to be at six on board the Resolution
in ease she should arrive before that
Jim

time. His Lordship went to dine at Mount Edge-
combe with Lord Edgecombe, The Resolution
did not come.
July ye 2d. Lord Sandwich left Plymouth with her
Yacht at six oilock in the morning.
We saw the Long Room & bathing place kept by
a few, who has fitted the same very neatly
up; but 2 believe there are so few people
to make use of it, that he scarcely will
find his act.
July 3. The Resolution was arrived in the night
we saw her from the Garrison, a fort
built of Stone on the top of La Rock
hill, which, commands the entrance
of the harbour; & is garrisoned by a
regiment of Soldiers. We went on board

regiment of Soldiers. We went on board
the Resolution Capt. Cook was gone
to the Yard: & we followed him there
& I went again on board the Ship with the
Carpenters & joiners in order to have all
& convenicacies
The Alteratiozes made, which I should
in the little Cabin allotted to me 4
wish for. After having given proper
directions, I returned to smooth &

18 ) dined after 5 o'clock, & as I could do nothing
on board till Tuesday or Wednesday, when the
afterations should be done, I took a
journey into Cornwall in order to see the
Finnines there.
July y
& Went with Mr Arnold & my son
to Saltash, having passed the Arm
of the Sea in a Ferry. The borough of Saltoph
lies on the Slope of a hill, & is upon the
whole but a sorry place, though it sends two
Members to Parliament. Passed Leskean & went to Bodmyn which formerly was a
Town of greater note, when the See of
a Bishoprich was there, so as I was told, though
I saw no remains of its ancient plea-
the ruins of

the ruins of
dor, except an old Church whose tower
still exists, & afford an habitation to owls & chapighs

July y.e We came to Lainivet in order to see
there some mines, which upon exasiation
nation, were only some wretched
Streamworks.
July 4th Resolved to go to Touro the only place
where I could expect mines: passed through

14
A Michael a poor little town: arrived at 12
docked at Souro. Which is a plan in every
thick condition. The Bank, the Custom
house, the Stamp office, & the houses
of some rich Merchants are elegant
buildings. Here the white Tin of the country
is stamped Each block of
Fix contains about 300 weight. I began
one Mr Beniele a man concerned in some
tinmines to give me directions & ic-

He sent his clerk one UN Marton with
who gave me a letter to Mrichard
williams at Poldice in Gwenap parish
Cornwall. It is about 6 miles west of Trare
The people brought one to the wrong man

The people brought one to the wrong man
for there are # Richard Williams, a Captain
of Mines, or Supervisors. the first I saw, was
at Kenwyn, in a new Tinwork. The
Tier is specheed into a greyish kind of Stone
called there Growan, & is a Mind of Saxum
or Moorstone or course Granite. The country or
wild Stone in which the vein runo case

vs. & west is chiefly Kellas or a kind of fibrous lamellated grey, greenish, or rare Stone of the As. best kind, which, combler into Singles, after having been exposed to the air for a good while. The vein is from B inches to 3 fattycoms wide & about 40 or 50 fathom deep. A great deal of Mundysk or Arseniculogite is in the via for road. Avale with a ribulet davider Kenwyn from Poddyte, where the drain is more valuable, being a fine white Quartz or as the mi- r Curasione miscall it bear with fine tin greains of the in it. but few/arsenical ore is in this kind: in some places of the vein, they find first Gozzum or a kind of an Iron ochre pot in such case they are always sure of finding Copper below it the Copper ore is a yellow Pyrites, which sometimes on account of the chinks & cracks of the ore had admitted the external air, & thus the ore begins decaying & forms those beautiful colours, which are like the pigeons neck & perfectly similar to the effects of an electrical Explosion upon metalo.

The vein at Polyce is from 8.0 to 105 deep,
is much richer, & increases in richness the
deeper it goes, but cannot be raised without
loss, on account of the immense expense
in raising the winter by the fire engined,
which are still contrived as the same
effect might be produced by half the coats,
if better contrive. She veins in Cornwall
for the greater part underlay North or dip
to the South; the load at Podque is just
the reverse, for it underlays South.
In all vales leading toward Tin veins, &
in places, where formerly the ancients
worked for Sin, small pieces of tingingus

worked for Sin, small pieces of tingingus
from the Size of a centil to that of a
wallput & even bigger are to be met with.
& these the minars Separate from the unyiel
ding stuff by washing, the light stuff going
away, when exposed to the current of a small
born, for which reason all the Tirquff got
in this manner is supposed to be
from
at well, of a wood when
a Streamwork. This Jury is as the ore
picked from the unagielding Stone, is brought
70

to a stampingmill & then washed & lastly sold in lots to those, who offer most for at, after having assay & the blacktin, as they call it. This blacktin they burn, if a great quantity of Mumbick is contained in the dresod black tin, in order to expel the arsenical & sulphureous particles, from it, & lastly they miss it with fleux & op 1st it in a furnace, to white tin; which is made into sloccks. of 300 weight, asayed & stamped. In the great Gauze-Moor, between Bodwyn & Michael is a great number of Streamworkers to the amount of 58, now actually working The minors, contrive several method, for to edge use trestirulets of this mood as streams For washing their Tenstuff, & likewise to work with it some pumps to clear the places under ground from water, where They are at work. In these Streamworks a kind of white Tinspar is found which the miners call Pryan, & which yields pure a King of Jin- In the common load of. Tin, there are sometime somewah at fibrous brown, blueish black your moagoes, called by the

Cornish Stannaries Black Jack, & is nothing but what
the German mineralogists call Wolfram or Sigma
lupi or a mixture of Iron & manganese;
& it must therefore carefully be distinguished from
the Substance called BlackJack by the Shropshire
Welsh & Derbyshire miners, which is a calam
blend. Having examined these mines, I thought
myself pretty well instructed of the Subject: &
returned first to Toura, & then to Bodmyn.
July ye 7.74
We saw the races at 13odmyn, four horses far
ted, once was distanceed; & the second heat tho

ed to Plymouth & found all things on board in
Ship for me done,
. 4.91.

& came on board, but the Ship was all in

4th
The Ships company was paid & the

confusing, & a regular fair was held on

ye 12.

July 4/2° when I was walking on the Quarter-
beck I found, that the Ship altered it.
lot
Situation in regard to the Adventure &
a Mast Ship, that were at anchor in

ned to one of the boys in the harbour;
We found nozo, that the cham by which
the boy was
fastened was broke & that the Ship

& moment all lands were on deck, the

kept ready to
grevent us from being

Our. Captain & all the Officers were ex-

just, when the Ship turned, She happily

passed between two rocks under water, & by the same manoeuvre the Ship nosed the Adventure, the Mast Ship, & the Isle of Nicholas; & there are dropt our anchor, heaving narrowly escaped, the most dangerous danger.

13th
July ye 13
We weighed anchor & left Plymouth
Pound in company with the Adventure at 4 o'
clock in the morning. The Adventure Sloop
was of 380 Tuns, Capt. Turneaux,& under him
Lieut. Shanks & Kempe. The Astronomer who
went on board of her was Mr Bailey. Our Ship
was of 469 Tuns both well rigged, stored & man-
ned & in all appearance the best matched
Ships for going on an Expedition, which
must of course require the greatest attention
as their success depended greatly on it,
& by all probability would become of great
importance, to Navigation, Geography
astronomy, & all the other branches

astronomy, & all the other branches
of Science. The Lords of the Admiralty &
Lord Sandwich
especially , had paid a particular attention
to the equipment of these ships & especially
to everything that bade fair to promote
the success of the Voyage & make the
whole Expedition as compleat as it could
be wished. As Mr Banks & Dr Solander had decli-
ned going on the Expedition, & the branches of

Natural History & the drawings of the various new objects which might offer on the course of this Voyage remained unoccupied Lord Sandwich appointed me for the one with my Son; & a few days before the Ship Sailed, Mr. Hodges an ingenious Artiller was sent to make up the things deficiency in the branch. In When we just had left Plymouth-Sound, I Souvia & his friend 100 Jones followed us in a small boat. They were making a li four into C.nicell as far as Fovey &.S. Aust. & were going to the first place by Sea, & wanted to take leave of us: they came on board of our Ship & after having breakfastes good tts with us, they took leave of us to date from, & we directed our under & N.W.b. W. wind course into the Shanhel. At 8 o'clock the Ramhead bore North, distant 2 miles. at 12 o'clock at noon the Latitude was observed 50° N. the Ramhead bore N.E 34 distant leagues. We passed Early Stone light house, it was about a leagues to reward

28
July 14° ) The Ships remained in company
the weather continued fair, the wind changed
to WSW & sometimes W. 6. N.N. nor course was SM. 4
S.W.b.S. In the Evening at 9 o'clock we bore
down to the Adventure. In the night the
weather became cloudy & towards morning
heavy with some rain at 8 in the morning
we fired a gun for the Adventure, after
having racked, directing our Course West
S.S. W. SWW. b.W towards noon it grew foggy
& only light airs.
July of 15th We met with a larvae Swell, from
the N.W. & towards night a thick fog come on
we soon found that we were approaching

we soon found that we were approaching
the Bay of Biscay, by the great waves. I had
been seasick ever since the first day of one
Voyage, could eat very little, was obliged to
throw up any Virtuals, had no head.each
I found squash much better & greatly
relieved by some mylled Portwine,
We tached on & off till
July 18, when we began to make our course directly
W.b. W. had some squalls & now & then rain

July
20 at half past 8 o'clock saw Cape Ortegal or as it should be called Ortequera bearing South
For 6 Leagues distant, at no on the ba me Lope
bore South about 2 or 3 leagues distant
The latitude was 43° 55. No. The Country
was very hilly, rovered with small wood & I believe, health, & we saw several pieces
of corn, in all appearance ripe:
21. We saw Cape Prior at 2 or 3 miles distance
bearing S. W. the weather happy & sometimes
rainly. at noon it was cloudy, however had
an Observation of the air 43° N. Groyn or Corunna SSE. & 2 Distant 3 Leagues
Cape Ortegal East. 12 or 14 leagues distant
ly no

ly no
We saw Groyn lighthouse. S.E.b.S. at 5 tea-
ques distance, sounded 87°. from. We went
at I o'clock on board a small Tartane
which we believed to be a Spanish
fishing Vessel in hopes to get fish, but
foods it to be a Turtan from Marseille

The high hills are very beautiful, & seem to be the produce of a Volcans, as the greater part of all the Cape Verd Isles seem likewise to be. The fine Exposure of the hill's birds pair make to the finest vineywards, & I am of opinion that the cool breezes from the sur roundin coran, would be sufficient, to keep the ornes from too great a heat, which might her & garden the skin of the grapes & hint their juice from being perfectly tempered digested by the Sun. But howsoever this be, there is no doubt, but this Isle, & all the Cape Verd Islie, would by cultivation, & the industry of a Set of rational planters Spring 4th all those productions which make the English West. Indies the source of wealth & richness; & more so, as the coast of Senegal & Guinea is so near at hand for fetching from thence, the necessary hands for cultivating these fine Islands

As this day early in the morning a heavy Shower of rain, had been falling, the poor Swalls which hitherto had been flying about the Ship, had its wings so much wetted, that it was unable to fly & was caught. My Sox nursed & dried the poor creature in his bosom, & then Set it at Liberty in the Peerage, where it immediately fed upon the numerous flies.
At Dinner it passed through the open port & retook its wondered liberty, & about six o clock it returned to its confinement, being sensible that we intended no harm to it: before Super it went out again & rose somewhere on the Ship during night. The next morning early the Swallow came in through the Quarter Gallery into the Cabin & afterwards into the Stecrage & took its breakfast of flies. after our break fast

We had now already serve to the Ships compe
ny the 2° Fat with Souer Kraut. or of
picked & fermented sliced Cabbage. This
food is extremely wholesome on account
of its antiseptic & antileorbatic quality

119
Oat as a fresh Vegetable & as an Acid. The Dolphins took
2 Vads of it in the first tour round the world, but
it was then disliked. In the 2d Voyage of the
Polphin it would not take among the
common Sailors. Capt Cook took some
in the Endinoour round the world; he found the
Ships company disliked it at first, but he
had it dressed every day for his table & per-
waded the Gentlemen in the Wardroom
to do the same, & had a Val, with the
head out, standing so that every body could

it: it was then found a good & palu-
table food & 2 Vals being thus gone: they

table food & 2 Vals being thus gone: they

when he got quite ulcerated Gums & the

Utility of wood in long Sea Voyages.

Otoo at last resolved to come into the Ship. There he got Capt. Farneaux presents, & soon went, off with Capt Farneaux & me, to the Resolution where his sister was, there he got likewise some presents & went off with the Captains to Opara. The morning I described some binds & Fish. went a shore, & shot a Duck & collected a fees plants. The next morning with daybreak we ever up & went into the country as far, as possible in one day. The march was very difficult, we went first on a hill, but finding it barren Shade & despite of woods, are passed a pale & another small hill & begun to, follow a fertile vali along which a rivalet rang. We were obligs to cross it several times. He ate his supper & went to bed, but seeing, that we had not yet supposed, he went up & sat up, till we went to bed.

When I had charged I came out of the pinnacle; the Fellow would not go begun a few Yards, when Capt Cook ordered him to be gone, but when he saw that I pointed my Gun at him he began to run off, as fast as nossible all the crowd dispersed & were frightened but we assured them, they were friends, & should no be invited; we only punished Chiefs & bad men which had the desired effect, & we began again to trade: in the mean while the boat was re- turning, & Capt. Cook sent the pinnacle for some marines with Arms, to protect the Trade whilst we stood a shore for them, we saw Mr sparmaca come gripped naked, & only wrapper an Ahoo with a blood on his head & another & the Shoulder & a third over his head. We kept the Anchor ready, & let it go in about no fathoms fine gravel-bottom. The Master who went alongshore with the boat & sounded, took a Native in & brought him on board. he was pretty brown, had fire punctured legs, was very hairy, had a shoot beard, & was covered only with a felt, from which a Kind of net hung down before his nudity. They gave him a hat & a jacket; the Coul in gave from some Medals, & I a nail & some beads with a ribbon which he wanted to have tied round his neck.

Journal of the Voyage resumed.
April y.e 22.d 1774 After we had moved the Ship, we
soon were surrounded by a great number of boats, who came
out of mere curiosity to see the Ship: They soon found
we were their old friends, & a little while after, they brought
a fish, breadfruit, Apples & Cocoanuts for trade & got
the
La douglasone
Nails in return. They had between two Canoes a kind of tongue
fixed both Ends secured with a kind of basket for the free Ent.
ce of the Water, & in it great Number of small alive
Mullets, which fried made a most excellent dish. We got like
wise some fine Bonitos.

after dinner I went ashore, & looked about, & found
the Country in every respect improved. There were a good
many hogs, many new houses were erected & numbers of new Canoes were building & already built, chiefly
owing to the facility they got. to build them by the
assamer of our tools of Iron. The Season we came in
was rainy, the rivers full of water, the country
even on the most distant hills covered with an a-
greeable verdure, & all plants grew in great luxu-
riancy. but we found no new ones, though an went pretty
high up a hill. My Son was now sick of the dry priper

high up a hill. My Son was now sick of the dry priper
as the Capt. M.r Whitehouse, & my Servt. & our cook
had been.
I went the next morning early on shore: &
crept over the Brookhill, & went to Opara: I found
a friendly Native, who went with me along.
I saw Tootaha's Mother & Anne, Too's Father,

10 miles in the Country, where my new friends grew was: He immediately roasted me breadfruit, & me de me a fine pudding, gave me Cocoanuts to drink & presented me with a fine Ahou, & I gave him a anife, a large Spike & beads; & to all his relations some small Nails, & then returned back & saw in my return near the Chiefs house the two goats Capt Furneaux gave to Otu; the She-goat had soon after kidded, & the 2 Kids were now nearly as big as the mother & both Spe's so that 17 the Natives take care the breed will soon be spread our all the Island, & become a valuable Acquisition especially if they brought them on the hills, which are the true peace for Goats. I saw new Tiatree, & took specimens with me, & offer-ved likewise a variety of the breadfruit Tree with narrow dissected leaves, which was an oblong & fruit. We went to Opara to see Otu, & likewise to see some War-Canes that jaane seen lying along shore. We saw about 44 of them, & Otu ordered some few men to make some warlike Evolutions before us, which they did with great dexterity. The Chiefs of the boats were all dressed & in some of them we saw even Chil- dren & very young headquartered, but none had helmets.

Tainamae told me the Child which she was going with, must be Killed as its father was an Arieidi, & therefore when I told, it was very bad to do so, she asked why so, I told that the Eutorn might be angry for doing so. She told the Bretannia Eatoon Se, but that there was not: I affirmed he was & at left that spevre not to illicit, if I gave a hatchet, a Shirt & a red Feather & came in time from Britannia to stitch the Child; A promise she never intend to keep, the three times as much were given to her. Next morning busy cooky we made an Exversion to the South of the were Jsl. He though many waters, & at last to a When,ooa or District called Bowheit on, where we found a Narai or legal oral Monument, called Tainco, & we were told it was Turnor's or Soopaea's Marai, & they affirmed strongly he was an aree, though last time when we were in Oriadea, we were told that Toopair was no Aree. The monument was 60 gands long & 5 wide, included by large flat stones of about 6 or 8 foot high & in the inside of this enclosure were small coralstones. We found there a new plant. The women exercise here the Out of Cooning, they gave me once a Pye made of Bananas & in its middle were leaves with Coormut Rernel, these leaves I suppose to be the OKmas or Shibibus con yophy Antue; ate another time, I got the Lehoes of a Fig. Tree, which I mid is woolly like a Peach & which they call gombaba, dressed in a Bahana-leaf & we found it a very fine leaf, & I often fashed them with the Natives raw. Their war of eating i.l. for they never touched the Virtuals with their Fingers, but to hold them in took allways from green leaves. And as the Ashes of the Volcano make all the leaves & trees, under which they must walk, very dirty, they find that it is impossible to Reize any thing slim between their Singers. When they got any thing leaves fron us, they took them likewise between two whether they use these leaves out of a principle of cleaning or out of Superstition, thinking everything coming from us either enchanted or morally unclear, I cannot decide. They did however use although leaves.

Journal of the voyage resumed
Sept y.e 1
We left now again off to search these
Seas, for farther discoveries, & return to colder de-
mates, & hope this last Expedition to the South will be
crowned with Success, & that we at last shall be restored
to the embraces of our Friends & Families, from whom
we have been long enough separated.

We examined the genitals of one of the Natives &

somewest Similar to the circumcision, viz: they

me showed me, that the Forshin was set & then stripp
backward over the glans.

Sept. 9. At dinner we ate but little, my Son grew sick, & throw all the portable Soup he had later. Mr. Sherman went again ashore & Jagain to bed, my head being giddy & my Limbo be-numbed, & this we continued all the afternoon, wiedrank some Sea & I had two Stools that night & felt a good appetite & had some Sago for Supper. There came several fridians on board that made signs, on being the sigh, that it was bald for the stomach & caused Sleep & Death; but we thought they wanted the Fish & I ordered the same to be offered to them, but they refused it with Condemn Signs of aversion, which proved they knew the Fish to be postpone & noxious. A little pig, I had gotten at Tana & which had catch all the garbage of the fish, when it was cleaned in the Evening before, was found dead this morning & the Dogs who had eaten the Liver, were likewise not well

10th The Natives came of as before, in boats & several came
swimming, they begin to grow fold of Iron, Nails, & gives,
hatchets & gimlets. The Chief got yesterday a Statehet of the
Capt with a Gimlet & a bog & a Witch; for he had sent Sugar-
Cane, & some Yanes; & I gave him there days before, when the Colops
was a very large Hatchet. at dinner George parted again
with his Peafe-Song: but the Capt. & I were somewhat better. Mr
Sparman was all this day employed in describing glaats, & my
on drew a few in the afternoon. I find that the pains will

on drew a few in the afternoon. I find that the pains will
likewise attack one, & my weakness in the Limbs & the Addiness
of the head is not yet over. If we find ourslars better to mor-
row, we intend to go ashore; & see what plants we can collect.
The next morning I went ashore & collected several new plants
For we found ourselves entirely better only a little weaker.
The Natives are the most harmless & goodnatured Set of people,
that we have as yet met with. They are poor on account
of the barrenness of their Soil, which does not afford them
many cultivated peants, & this makes that they cannot

many cultivated peants, & this makes that they cannot
give any away to us. Their houses are constructed over a smelton
of sticks in the Shape of a behave on the inside they
sis to be a coating of Discount mats o quite on the inside
on other coating of the bark of Cayputi Tree; on the side is a thick
thatch of a kind of grass, diet as we were so little ashore
on account of aue. illness, & had enough to do with collecting
plants, & did not understand much of their lan-
guage, we could not learn which gave they employ for the
purpose & for manufacturing their ropes & the patients

purpose & for manufacturing their ropes & the patients
of their Women. They have but a few Cocoanuts & I saw a

dress & wrench, by which operation a thin Shin goes off & a hand of pulp is set free from the outer kind. The pulp is eaten & the string sweet kind they sick. I could never make them understand,what plant it was they used in that nan->ner. They eat a great man, Shelpigh which they constantly ol-lect, on their reefs. They have not other animal food, but That the Sea afood them, & as I believe but sparingly the this are very expertions, at striking Fish with their Spears. We left them the Dog & a bitch, & if nothing intervenes a little Borar & a Bow are intended to be left here as a present; & if the breeds of these a kinds of Anime succeed it must give them an easier animal food & a greater Veriety of it. Our goat has hidde a few days ago, but both kids died. And these Kids lived long enough & has learn to eat grass, we might have a chance to set them somewhere in the South-Sea ashore & to raise them a bread of Goats: but we have been infortunate in that respect. One of the Goats we bought at St Yago died of the Survey, so did the goat relong to the Gentlemen in the Gunnom. A Goat & He got ashore in N. Zealand was still Milled by the Natives, a young he got we had, fell over board & was drowned; & the old the Goat we ate something in Nzeeland, which may him so mad, that he ran in the Sea & perished thereon.

Having dropt our Anchor, Boats, were hoisted out
& we went ashore: It rained several Squalls. We found on the
Rocks, all along the Shore of the Island, vast numbers of a kind
of Seal, which deserves to be called the Sea Lion worth
far greater Justice, than that observed by Lord arson in his Vogue
at Juan Fernandez, for the Males of this our Seal have
all a Shaggy long kind of their, from the head down to
the Shoutber & fore-Tino; the Configuration of the head
in the Females is somewhat related to that of a Linneg
& the tawny somewhat yellowish colour of their Huir

& the tawny somewhat yellowish colour of their Huir
all contribute to bring a melation about this Seal & the Lion. The largest we saw more
from 12 to 15' Feet one
The Lionnesses from 6 to 89. One of the middling Sales
after the Skin, Entrails & Blubber were taken away
weighed 550 & weight; so that the largest of whom
certainly must have weighed 1000 or 1200 ft. They shoot
& row, are polygamous, have one or two Cubs at a time
the Females fleet like Calves, & the Cubs like Lambs.
They lie in Families together, with the young Cubs
& such young ones as are not fit for propagation.

& such young ones as are not fit for propagation.
Dr. Masses lie Separate, are very toobid & indolent
but fierce, peevish & cruel. None of the others map
approach their nesting-place, or else they must fight.
I saw some with many gashes on their back; & when
the Fight they seize one another, on the Black
& Fidls' with a rage & fury hardly to be described.

We had a fresh wind & approached soon the Isle of St.
george, having past the Eastermost Extremity of Fayal.
which is high & abluff point. Pico seemed to have a gentle
Hope, except where the Peak begins, which almost terminates
in a point. I George has step & bold Stores & was only
on top cultivated. its NW. point looked much like
Point Jackson in N. Zeeland. Graciosa has a little Rock
to Leward, & is gently sleeping. When we came undots
& George, the Isle broke the wind & we were near
proclaimed. The Thermometer at 73°

after 2 hours walk & being wet to the Ship from a South
Thower, & climbing up 2 hills & crossing 2 deep valleys, I
came to a small hot where a man, his Wife & 3th
been lived in. I promised the Man presents in close
he would go up with me next day up the hill's
give me a night holding & provitude some Cocoanuts
& baked breadfruit: he agreed to it, & in consequence we
took our quarters in his hut, which he immediately
enlarged; We supposed here & got as went to bed, which
2 others were keeping watch, & after 10 o'clock we re-
